questions.q: What is the primary difference between a Docker image and a Docker container?
options: An image is a blueprint or template; a container is a running instance of that image with its own isolated environment
An image runs in memory while a container is stored on disk
Images are used for testing and containers are used for production
A container is a lighter version of an image that uses fewer resources
questions.explanation: A Docker image is an immutable template containing all dependencies and code; a container is a live, isolated execution environment created from that image. Memory/disk distinction (B) is wrong—both run in memory; testing/production (C) is a usage pattern, not a technical difference; size/resource differences (D) don't define the relationship.

questions.q: What are Docker layers, and why are they important?
options: Layers are stacked filesystem snapshots in a Dockerfile; each instruction creates a layer, enabling reuse and reducing image size through caching
Layers are separate servers that host different parts of the application
Layers are security boundaries that isolate containers from the host system
Layers are version control commits for Docker images
questions.explanation: Each Dockerfile instruction creates a layer, and Docker caches them to speed up rebuilds and reduce redundant work. Layers aren't servers (B), security boundaries (C), or version commits (D)—they're the core of Docker's efficiency model.

options: Defines the steps and configuration to build a Docker image automatically
Specifies how to connect containers to a network
Manages permissions for files inside a container
Schedules when containers should start and stop
questions.explanation: A Dockerfile contains instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, etc.) that Docker uses to build images layer by layer. Network configuration (B), file permissions (C), and scheduling (D) are separate concerns handled by other Docker features.

options: A mechanism for persistent data storage and sharing between containers and the host, surviving container restarts
The maximum amount of data a container can process at once
A compressed archive of a Docker image for faster distribution
A configuration file that lists all running containers
questions.explanation: Volumes persist data outside container lifecycles and enable container-to-host and container-to-container data sharing. Data capacity (B), image compression (C), and container lists (D) are unrelated to volumes' purpose.

questions.q: What problem does Docker container networking solve?
options: Enabling containers to communicate with each other and the host while maintaining isolation and service discovery
Making containers run faster by reducing network latency
Replacing physical network infrastructure
Preventing all containers from accessing the internet
questions.explanation: Docker networks allow inter-container communication and hostname-based service discovery while keeping containers isolated. Network speed (B) is a side effect, not the problem solved; Docker doesn't replace physical networks (C); it doesn't block internet access (D).

questions.q: Why is the FROM instruction typically the first instruction in a Dockerfile?
options: It specifies the base image that all other layers build upon
It defines the starting network configuration for the container
It sets the version of Docker to use for building
It marks the beginning of the build process chronologically
questions.explanation: FROM establishes the base image (OS, runtime, libraries) that subsequent instructions modify and extend. Network config (B) comes later; Docker version (C) is configured elsewhere; chronological ordering (D) is less important than the logical dependency on a base image.

questions.q: What is the advantage of using .dockerignore in a project?
options: To exclude files from being copied into the image during build, reducing image size and build time
To prevent containers from accessing certain files on the host
To ignore error messages during container execution
To skip security scanning of specific files
questions.explanation: .dockerignore prevents unnecessary files (like node_modules, .git, test files) from being added to images, improving build speed and reducing bloat. It's not about host file access (B), error handling (C), or security scans (D)—it's a build optimization.

questions.q: What happens to data in a container when it stops or is deleted without using a volume?
options: The data is lost because it exists only in the container's writable layer
The data is automatically backed up to the host system
The data persists indefinitely until manually deleted
The data is transferred to a default Docker archive location
questions.explanation: Container data without volumes is ephemeral—it's destroyed when the container stops. Backups don't happen automatically (B); data doesn't persist without volumes (C); Docker doesn't archive container data by default (D). This is why volumes are essential for stateful applications.

questions.q: In a Dockerfile, what does the EXPOSE instruction do?
options: Documents which ports the container listens on; it does not actually publish ports without additional runtime flags
Opens ports on the host machine automatically
Encrypts network traffic on specified ports
Lists all exposed vulnerabilities in the base image
questions.explanation: EXPOSE is metadata documenting container ports but doesn't publish them—actual port binding requires -p flags at runtime. It doesn't open host ports (B), encrypt traffic (C), or scan vulnerabilities (D)—it's purely for documentation.

questions.q: What is a 'multi-stage build' in Docker?
options: Using multiple FROM instructions to build an image in stages, keeping only artifacts from final stages to reduce image size
Building the same image multiple times for testing purposes
Deploying containers across multiple cloud providers in sequence
Running multiple containers in parallel during the build process
questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM instructions to build intermediate images, copying only necessary artifacts to the final image, dramatically reducing size. Testing repeats (B), multi-cloud deployment (C), and parallel builds (D) are separate concerns unrelated to multi-stage builds.
